source.title: Directed evolution of phosphite dehydrogenase to cycle noncanonical redox cofactors via universal growth selection platform

initial_request.research_problem: Engineering enzymes to recycle noncanonical redox cofactors like NMN+ for orthogonal biocatalysis, hindered by lack of high-throughput selection methods.
initial_request.known_context: Noncanonical cofactors enable orthogonal redox reactions but enzymes lack efficient activity. Growth-coupled selection could enable directed evolution if a suitable selection strain is engineered.
constraints: Must use SHuffle strain (Δgor ΔtrxB) for oxidative stress selection
Must engineer Gor to be NMNH-specific to couple growth to NMN+ reduction
input_data: Crystal structures of Gor and PTDH
Known mutations R198M-R204L in Gor that abolish NADPH activity
quantifiable_goals: Increase catalytic efficiency (kcat/KM) of PTDH for NMN+
Achieve >99% conversion in biocatalytic cycling with NMN+

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No high-throughput selection method for NMN+-reducing enzymes.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can E. coli SHuffle strain be engineered to require NMNH-dependent Gor for survival under oxidative stress?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: E. coli SHuffle strain (Δgor ΔtrxB) requires functional Gor to survive oxidative stress from diamide, enabling growth-coupled selection for NMN+-reducing enzymes if Gor is engineered to use NMNH.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Proceed to engineer Gor for NMNH specificity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated based on known SHuffle strain properties.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to design mutations in Gor to accept NMNH.

evidence.quote_or_span: our hypothesis is that it cannot combat the oxidative stress effectively, and requires a functional Gor to survive exposure to oxidants such as diamide

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Gor naturally uses NADPH; NMNH lacks AMP moiety.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to engineer Gor to recognize NMNH instead of NADPH?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Ribose and phosphate groups of NMN(H) can accept new polar interactions to replace missing AMP interactions in Gor.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Target residues near NMNH phosphate for mutagenesis.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis that new interactions can compensate for lost AMP contacts.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design specific mutations at G174, G176, I178.

evidence.quote_or_span: We hypothesized that the ribose and phosphate groups of NMN(H) have the potential to accept new polar interactions to supplant the missing interactions from the AMP recognition handle.
Three residue sites G174, G176, and I178 were targeted based on their proximity to the ribose-phosphate group of NMN(H)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No Gor mutants with NMNH activity.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which residues to mutate to create NMNH interactions?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mutations at G174, G176, I178 near NMNH phosphate will enhance NMNH binding.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Create and test single mutants at these positions.

tool_or_method.name: Rational design based on crystal structure

evolution_trajectory.observation: I178T predicted to form favorable interaction with NMNH phosphate.

evidence.quote_or_span: Three residue sites G174, G176, and I178 were targeted based on their proximity to the ribose-phosphate group of NMN(H)
Combining the R198M-R204L double mutations on top of Ec Gor I178T caused only a modest decrease in NMNH-dependent activity

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Wild-type Gor has negligible NMNH activity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure NMNH-dependent activity of I178T mutant.
evolution_trajectory.observation: I178T showed 4-fold enhanced NMNH-dependent activity compared to wild-type Gor.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Combine I178T with NADPH-abolishing mutations to create NMNH-specific Gor.

evidence.quote_or_span: I178T had 4-fold enhanced NMNH-dependent activity compared to the wild type
Indeed, I178T had 4-fold enhanced NMNH-dependent activity compared to the wild type.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: I178T has improved NMNH activity but still uses NADPH.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to eliminate NADPH activity while retaining NMNH activity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Combining I178T with R198M-R204L will drastically reduce NADPH activity while maintaining NMNH activity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Gor Ortho had ~15,000-fold reduction in NADPH catalytic efficiency and ~9,000-fold reduction vs I178T, with modest decrease in NMNH activity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate growth selection platform using Gor Ortho.

evidence.quote_or_span: Combining the R198M-R204L double mutations on top of Ec Gor I178T caused only a modest decrease in NMNH-dependent activity

Importantly, the triple mutant Ec Gor I178T-R198M-R204L (named Gor Ortho) has drastically reduced catalytic efficiency for NADPH, by \~15,000-fold and \~9000-fold, respectively, compared to Ec Gor wild type and I178T single mutant (Fig.
Combining the R198M-R204L double mutations on top of Ec Gor I178T caused only a modest decrease in NMNH-dependent activity (Fig.

Furthermore, a previously identified variant with R198M-R204L double mutations had almost completely abolished NADPH-dependent activity, possibly due to ablation of interactions with the 2'-phosphate group on NADPH $^{38}$ (Fig.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Wild-type TS-PTDH has low NMN+ activity.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can rational design improve PTDH activity toward NMN+?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mutations near the adenine pocket will enhance NMN+ binding.
evolution_trajectory.observation: A155N showed ~3-fold increase in specific activity and catalytic efficiency for NMN+; T101K-A155G showed ~2-fold increase.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Use A155N as template for directed evolution.

evidence.quote_or_span: Through specific activity assay we identified two variants of interest displaying enhanced NMN $^{+}$ -dependent activity, A155N and T101K-A155G.
A155N showed \~3-fold increase and T101K-A155G showed a \~2-fold increase compared to the wild type.
4$ nmol min $^{-1}$ mg $^{-1}$ , while T101K-A155G showed roughly twofold, and A155N around threefold increase (Fig.
Further kinetic analysis of A155N indicated a \~3-fold increase in catalytic efficiency for NMN $^{+}$ compared to TS-PTDH (Table 1).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which additional residues to target for further improvement?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Residues E175, A176, L208 in the adenine cleft can be mutated to enhance NMN+ binding.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct site-saturation mutagenesis library on A155N targeting E175, A176, L208.

evidence.quote_or_span: The PTDH combinatorial site-saturation mutagenesis library pLZ316 was constructed with degenerate codon-containing primers.
The library size of pLZ316 was counted as $4.7 \times 10^{7}$ transformants.
We next constructed a site-saturated mutagenesis library on top of TS-PTDH A155N targeting E175, A176, and L208.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can growth selection enrich variants with improved NMN+ activity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Growth under diamide stress with NMN+ will select for PTDH variants with higher NMN+ reduction activity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform first round growth selection with 0.5 mM diamide, 5 g/L yeast extract, 5 mM NMN+.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 8 colonies obtained; LY-6 (A155N-E175Q-A176S) and LY-7 (A155N-E175W-A176G-L208V) showed 17-fold and 11-fold increased catalytic efficiency for NMN+ vs A155N.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Increase selection stringency to further improve activity.

evidence.quote_or_span: A total of 50 $\mu$ L pre-made SHuffle/pLZ311 or SHuffle/pLZ312 electrocompetent cells were transformed with 2 $\mu$ L TS-PTDH A155N-E175-A176-L208 NNK library DNA (pLZ316) via electroporation.
The independent transformants sampled was calculated as 2.4 × 10⁶ and 2.5 × 10⁶ colonies when plated on 5 and 1 g/L yeast extract, respectively.

Among them, TS-PTDH A155N-E175Q-A176S (LY-6) and TS-PTDH A155N-E175W-A176G-L208V (LY-7) exhibited the greatest improvement, with \~17-fold and \~11-fold increased catalytic efficiency ( $k_{cat}/K_{M}$ ) for NMN $^{+}$ compared to the parent enzyme TS-PTDH A155N, respectively (Table 1).
From the 8 colonies, four PTDH variants were isolated that showed enhanced NMN $^{+}$ -dependent activity compared to the template TS-PTDH A155N (data not shown).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: LY-6 and LY-7 isolated with improved activity.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can further stringency yield even better variants?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Reducing yeast extract to 1 g/L will increase selection pressure for higher NMN+ activity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform second round growth selection with 1 g/L yeast extract.
evolution_trajectory.observation: LY-13 (A155N-E175A-A176F) isolated with 110-fold improvement over TS-PTDH and 44-fold over A155N in catalytic efficiency (0.44 mM⁻¹ s⁻¹).

evidence.quote_or_span: Using the same growth selection process, we successfully isolated TS-PTDH A155N-E175A-A176F (LY-13) which has a catalytic efficiency for $NMN^{+}$ \~110-fold and \~44-fold higher than that of TS-PTDH and TS-PTDH A155N, respectively, reaching $0.

Using the same growth selection process, we successfully isolated TS-PTDH A155N-E175A-A176F (LY-13) which has a catalytic efficiency for $NMN^{+}$ \~110-fold and \~44-fold higher than that of TS-PTDH and TS-PTDH A155N, respectively, reaching $0.44\ mM^{-1}\ s^{-1}$ (Table 1).

Among them, TS-PTDH A155N-E175Q-A176S (LY-6) and TS-PTDH A155N-E175W-A176G-L208V (LY-7) exhibited the greatest improvement, with \~17-fold and \~11-fold increased catalytic efficiency ( $k_{cat}/K_{M}$ ) for NMN $^{+}$ compared to the parent enzyme TS-PTDH A155N, respectively (Table 1).
In particular, LY-6 utilized a hydrogen bond network to brace the AMP binding pocket, which is in stark contrast with the hydrophobic packing mode utilized by LY-7 and LY-13 (Fig.

When BNA $^{+}$ was reduced with wild type TS-PTDH, LY-7, and LY-13, a cofactor dose-dependent response in biotransformation efficiency was observed, and at 20 mM BNA $^{+}$ supplementation, LY-7 and LY-13 showed improved utilization of the BNA $^{+}$ relative to the wild type protein (Fig.
LY-13 (A155N-E175A-A176F) displays similar hydrophobic packing in the adenine cleft as LY-7 with E175A shrinking and A176F settling against L208 and P209 in the adenine cleft.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: LY-6, LY-7, LY-13 isolated with unknown structural mechanisms.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How do mutations in evolved variants enhance NMN+ activity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mutations recapitulate AMP binding via hydrophobic packing or hydrogen bond networks, inducing closed conformation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform structural modeling of LY-6, LY-7, LY-13.
evolution_trajectory.observation: LY-6 uses hydrogen bond network; LY-7 and LY-13 use hydrophobic packing to bridge adenine cleft, mimicking AMP interactions.

evidence.quote_or_span: In LY-6 (A155N-E175Q-A176S), E175Q maintains similar steric properties of the original glutamate, but the change from the carboxylic acid to amide enables E175Q to act as a hydrogen bond donor that bridges separate loops in the adenine binding cleft for improved stability.
LY-7 (A155N-E175W-A176G-L208V) is characterized by dense hydrophobic packing in the adenine cleft.
LY-13 (A155N-E175A-A176F) displays similar hydrophobic packing in the adenine cleft as LY-7 with E175A shrinking and A176F settling against L208 and P209 in the adenine cleft.

Both LY-7 (A155N-E175W-A176G-L208V) and LY-13 (A155N-E175A-A176F) display tight hydrophobic packing that bridges the gap between the loop emerging from the second Rossman beta strand and the loop below the bound cofactor.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: LY-13 has high catalytic efficiency but not tested in biocatalytic cycling.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can LY-13 efficiently recycle NMN+ in a coupled reaction?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: LY-13 will enable high conversion in NMN+ cycling with XenA for ketoisophorone reduction.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test LY-13 in NMN+ cycling with XenA and also with BNA+ for levodione production.
evolution_trajectory.observation: >99% conversion at 0.5 mM NMN+; LY-13 also recycles BNA+ for levodione production.

evidence.quote_or_span: TTN was calculated by the moles of levodione produced divided by the moles of LY-13 supplied to the reaction system.

When BNA $^{+}$ was reduced with wild type TS-PTDH, LY-7, and LY-13, a cofactor dose-dependent response in biotransformation efficiency was observed, and at 20 mM BNA $^{+}$ supplementation, LY-7 and LY-13 showed improved utilization of the BNA $^{+}$ relative to the wild type protein (Fig.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Growth selection platform and evolved PTDH variants demonstrated.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What are the limitations of the growth-based selection approach?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Growth-based selection requires case-by-case metabolic tailoring and fine-tuning catalytic parameters for non-canonical cofactors remains difficult.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Growth-based selection requires case-by-case metabolic tailoring; fine-tuning catalytic parameters for non-canonical cofactors remains difficult.

evidence.quote_or_span: The main bottleneck in developing growth-based selections is that cell metabolism must be tailored case-by-case to make the desired enzymatic activity essential for cell survival.
fine tuning these specific parameters has largely proven difficult, especially when engineering for novel activity with non-canonical cofactors

validation_methods: Kinetic assays for NMN+ and NADPH activity
Biocatalytic cycling with XenA and BNA+
success_verification.final_conclusion: Directed evolution using a growth selection platform yielded PTDH variant LY-13 with 110-fold improved catalytic efficiency for NMN+, enabling >99% conversion in biocatalytic cycling.
limitations: Growth-based selection requires case-by-case metabolic tailoring
Fine-tuning catalytic parameters for non-canonical cofactors remains difficult
